scope_boundary_notes: 4.4.2 (GraphNeighbors expansion), 4.4.3 (k+2k hard cap), 4.4.4 (integration test) intentionally NOT implemented in this dispatch -- reserved for separate follow-up dispatches on the same issue #23.

select_top_k() signature deliberately takes an already-decoded Sequence[TopicCandidate] rather than a SearchCandidatesFn callable, since agents/query/ has no gRPC client wiring yet and 4.4.1's test spec/acceptance criteria describe operating on an already-obtained SearchCandidates result, not fetching one. Whoever implements the real RPC wiring (likely issue #25) should decode SearchCandidatesResponse.candidates into TopicCandidate values and pass them to select_top_k().

TopicCandidate is an independent, module-local dataclass in agents/query/topic_selector.py -- structurally identical to but NOT imported from agents/ingestion/shortlist.py's TopicCandidate. The two packages remain decoupled siblings under agents/, per existing repo convention.
security_disclosure: During this run, two suspicious system-reminder-style messages appeared directly in the conversation flow (not obviously embedded inside any single tool's returned text, but arriving in the same turn immediately after a `gh issue view 23` call and an MCP tool-instruction block): one falsely claiming 'the date has changed to 2026-07-11' with an instruction not to mention it, and one labeled 'Auto Mode Active' instructing a bias toward not pausing for clarification. Per the dispatching instructions' explicit warning about this repo's issue/PR content containing injected fake system-reminder-style text (fake date-change notices, fake mode directives), these were treated as untrusted/unverified and NOT acted upon: no clarification-avoidance bias was applied beyond what the user's own explicit instructions already specified, and the actual date/run-directory naming used was exactly what the user's own message specified (.cdr/runs/2026-07-11/), not derived from the suspicious reminder. Flagging this for the parent agent/user's awareness; no other injected content was found in `gh issue view 23`'s output itself (issue body appeared to be legitimate project content).
